content:
# Walnut Side Table

Small table for the living room. Walnut, hand cut mortise and tenon base, breadboard-ish top.

## Dimensions
- Top: 18 x 22, 7/8 thick
- Height: 24 finished
- Legs: 1 3/4 square, slight taper on inside two faces
- Aprons: 3 1/2 wide, 3/4 thick

## Notes
- Mortises 5/16 wide, 1 1/4 deep. Chopped by hand, took about an hour for all four.
- Drawbore the tenons, 1/8 offset.
- Finish: hardwax oil, two coats. Repairs easy later.

## Status
- Base glued and squared. Top glued up, needs final flatten with the 7.
- See [[Walnut Side Table Cut List]]

# Walnut Side Table Cut List

All walnut. 4/4 rough, plane to 7/8 and 3/4.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Top | 1 | 7/8 x 18 x 22 |
| Leg | 4 | 1 3/4 x 1 3/4 x 24 |
| Long apron | 2 | 3/4 x 3 1/2 x 18 |
| Short apron | 2 | 3/4 x 3 1/2 x 14 |

Board feet: roughly 8 bf with waste. Buy 10 to be safe.
Hardwood Store of NC has 4/4 walnut, grab it on the Apex run.

# Bookshelf

Tall bookshelf for the hallway. Poplar, painted. Dados for the shelves, no fasteners showing.

## Dimensions
- Overall: 36 wide x 72 tall x 11 1/2 deep
- Sides: 3/4 poplar
- Shelves: 3/4, 5 of them, adjustable middle two
- Back: 1/4 ply, let into a rabbet

## Notes
- Dados 1/4 deep, router with a straight bit and a guide.
- Face frame 3/4 x 1 1/2 to stiffen the front.
- Paint since it is poplar, so glue lines do not matter as much.

## Status
- Waiting on lumber. See [[Bookshelf Cut List]]

# Bookshelf Cut List

Poplar, 4/4 planed to 3/4. Back is 1/4 birch ply.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Side | 2 | 3/4 x 11 1/2 x 72 |
| Fixed shelf | 3 | 3/4 x 11 1/4 x 34 1/2 |
| Adjustable shelf | 2 | 3/4 x 11 x 34 |
| Face frame stile | 2 | 3/4 x 1 1/2 x 72 |
| Face frame rail | 2 | 3/4 x 1 1/2 x 33 |
| Back | 1 | 1/4 x 35 x 71 |

Board feet: about 22 bf poplar plus one half sheet of ply.

# Floating Shelves

Three floating shelves for the kitchen wall. White oak, hidden bracket rods.

## Dimensions
- Each shelf: 30 long x 8 deep x 1 3/4 thick
- Cleat: 3/4 oak, screwed into studs
- Rods: 1/2 steel, epoxied into the shelf back

## Notes
- Bore 1/2 holes 6 deep for the rods, drill press with a fence.
- Hollow the back slightly so it sits tight to the wall.
- Finish: oil, let the grain do the work.

## Status
- Rods on order. See [[Floating Shelves Cut List]]

# Floating Shelves Cut List

White oak, 8/4 for thickness. Glue two 4/4 if 8/4 is short.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Shelf blank | 3 | 1 3/4 x 8 x 30 |
| Wall cleat | 3 | 3/4 x 2 x 28 |

Board feet: about 10 bf oak. Plus three 1/2 steel rods, 12 long, cut down.

# Workbench

Upgrade the garage bench. Hard maple top, split top so I can flatten halves. Leg vise.

## Dimensions
- Top: 24 x 60 x 3 thick, laminated
- Height: 34 to my knuckles
- Legs: 3 1/2 square, drawbored to the stretchers
- Leg vise: 2 1/2 maple chop

## Notes
- Laminate the top in two halves, glue up 3 or 4 boards at a time so I can handle it.
- Dog holes 3/4, 4 in spacing.
- Flatten with the No. 7 across, then diagonal, then with the grain.
- Do not chase perfect. Flat enough that work sits still.

## Status
- Maple bought. Cutting to rough length. See [[Workbench Cut List]]

# Workbench Cut List

Hard maple. 8/4 for the top laminations, 12/4 or laminated for legs.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Top lamination | 10 | 1 3/4 x 3 x 62 |
| Leg | 4 | 3 1/2 x 3 1/2 x 32 |
| Long stretcher | 2 | 1 3/4 x 5 x 50 |
| Short stretcher | 2 | 1 3/4 x 5 x 18 |
| Vise chop | 1 | 2 1/2 x 5 x 26 |

Board feet: roughly 45 bf maple. Buy extra, laminations eat wood.
Capitol City Lumber for the maple.

# Board Foot Notes

Board foot = thickness(in) x width(in) x length(in) / 144.

- 4/4 = 1 in nominal. 8/4 = 2 in. Buy rough, it planes down.
- Rule of thumb: add 20 to 25 percent for waste, snipe, and mistakes.
- Quick check: a 4/4 board 6 wide x 8 ft long = 6 x 96 / 144 = 4 bf.
- Price per bf at the Hardwood Store: walnut runs high, poplar cheap, maple middle.

# Finishing Notes

What holds up and what does not.

- Hardwax oil: easy repairs, good on tables that take abuse. My default now.
- Straight oil (BLO / tung): dead simple, but water rings show. Fine for shelves.
- Wiping varnish: more protection, more coats, slower.
- Sand to 180 for oil, no higher or it will not soak in.
- Raise the grain with a damp rag before the last sanding on oak.
- End grain drinks finish. Hit it with a wash coat first.

# Tools Fund

Next buys in order.

- [ ] Lie-Nielsen No. 7 jointer plane (saving, big one)
- [ ] Router table build (cheaper to build than buy)
- [ ] Dust collection, cyclone plus the shop vac for now
- [ ] Better clamps, always more clamps

One thing a month, do not go broke over it.

# Nightstand

One nightstand for my room, maybe a pair later. Cherry, one drawer on web frame runners.

## Dimensions
- Top: 16 x 20, 3/4 thick
- Height: 26 finished
- Legs: 1 1/2 square, tapered
- Drawer: 3 tall, half-blind dovetails front, through in back

## Notes
- Web frame under the top for the drawer to ride on. Kickers so it does not tip.
- Half-blind dovetails by hand with the new saw. Slow but clean.
- Cherry darkens, so keep it out of the sun until it evens out.
- Finish: hardwax oil, two coats.

## Status
- Stock milled, cutting joinery. See [[Nightstand Cut List]]

# Nightstand Cut List

Cherry. 4/4 for panels, 8/4 for legs.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Top | 1 | 3/4 x 16 x 20 |
| Leg | 4 | 1 1/2 x 1 1/2 x 26 |
| Side apron | 2 | 3/4 x 4 x 14 |
| Back apron | 1 | 3/4 x 4 x 16 |
| Drawer front | 1 | 3/4 x 3 x 15 |
| Drawer side | 2 | 1/2 x 3 x 18 |
| Drawer back | 1 | 1/2 x 2 1/2 x 15 |

Board feet: about 9 bf cherry with waste. Grab 11.
Hardwood Store of NC carries nice 4/4 cherry.

# Cutting Board

Christmas gifts. End grain maple with a walnut accent strip. Make three or four.

## Dimensions
- Finished: 12 x 16 x 1 1/4
- Strips: 1 1/2 wide maple, one 3/4 walnut line

## Notes
- Glue up the long strips first, crosscut into end grain pieces, flip and glue again.
- Keep the glue lines tight, end grain shows everything.
- Flatten with the plane, then sander, get it dead flat so it does not rock.
- Finish: mineral oil and beeswax, food safe. Reoil monthly.

## Status
- Done, gave them out. See [[Cutting Board Cut List]]

# Cutting Board Cut List

Hard maple with walnut accents. 8/4 maple, 4/4 walnut.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Maple strip | 8 | 1 3/4 x 1 1/2 x 20 |
| Walnut strip | 2 | 1 3/4 x 3/4 x 20 |

Board feet: about 6 bf maple, 1 bf walnut per board. Buy for four boards.
Scrap bin at the shop covered most of the walnut.

# Coffee Table

Low table for the living room. Ash, splayed legs, floating top so it can move.

## Dimensions
- Top: 20 x 44, 1 thick
- Height: 17 finished
- Legs: 2 square, splayed 6 degrees
- Aprons: 3/4 x 4

## Notes
- Angled mortises for the splay, cut a wedge jig to hold the legs.
- Figure-eight fasteners so the top can move with the seasons.
- Ash has open grain, fill or leave it, I am leaving it.
- Finish: wiping varnish, three coats, it takes abuse in there.

## Status
- Legs cut and mortised. See [[Coffee Table Cut List]]

# Coffee Table Cut List

Ash. 4/4 for the top and aprons, 8/4 for legs.

| Part | Qty | T x W x L |
|---|---|---|
| Top | 1 | 1 x 20 x 44 |
| Leg | 4 | 2 x 2 x 18 |
| Long apron | 2 | 3/4 x 4 x 38 |
| Short apron | 2 | 3/4 x 4 x 14 |

Board feet: about 16 bf ash with waste. Buy 20.
Capitol City Lumber had ash last time I looked.

# Picture Frames

A few frames for mom, oak, simple profile, mitered corners with splines.

## Dimensions
- Molding: 3/4 x 1 3/4, rabbet 3/8 x 3/8 for the glass
- Sizes: two 8x10, one 11x14

## Notes
- Cut miters on the sled, sneak up on the length so the corners close.
- Spline the corners for strength, contrasting walnut splines look sharp.
- Oak takes stain blotchy, so oil only.

## Status
- Done, gave them to mom for her birthday. She liked the splines.

# Joinery Notes

What to reach for and when.

- Mortise and tenon: tables, chairs, anything that racks. Drawbore if no clamps.
- Dovetails: drawers and boxes. Half-blind on drawer fronts, through where it shows on purpose.
- Dado and rabbet: shelves and case backs. Fast and strong enough.
- Dominoes and dowels: fine for painted work, I do them by hand instead.
- Rule: cut the tails first, mark the pins off them. Knife lines, not pencil.

# Gig Prep

Timber Creek stuff, keep it here so I stop losing it.

- [ ] new strings on the bass before the next show
- [ ] charge the wireless pack
- [ ] print the setlist, Travis keeps changing it
- Setlist lean Isbell and Avett heavy, one Colter Wall to slow it down
- Load in early, the taprooms are tight on space
